description.en: A 1,200 km circular pilgrimage visiting 88 Buddhist temples around the island of Shikoku, following the footsteps of the monk Kūkai (Kōbō Daishi). One of the few circular pilgrimages in the world.
description.ja: 弘法大師空海の足跡をたどり、四国の88の寺院を巡る約1,200kmの巡礼路。

waypointCoverageNote.en: 20 of 88 temples included as representative waypoints. Full 88-temple dataset planned for a future release.

origin.en: The pilgrimage traces the path of the monk Kūkai (774-835), founder of Shingon Buddhism in Japan. Kūkai practiced asceticism across Shikoku before founding the monastic center at Mount Kōya. The 88-temple circuit was formalized during the Edo period (1603-1868). The phrase 'Dōgyō Ninin' (walking with two) inscribed on the pilgrim's hat signifies that Kōbō Daishi walks with every pilgrim.

description.en: A calligraphy book purchased at Temple 1 (2,000-3,500 JPY). At each temple, monks write the temple name, principal deity, and temple number in calligraphy and apply three red stamps. Cost: 500 JPY per temple (44,000 JPY total). Stamp hours: 7:00-17:00.
'Walking with Two' — the belief that Kōbō Daishi walks alongside every pilgrim. Inscribed on the sedge hat. The pilgrim is never alone.

Traditional pilgrim outfit: white jacket (hakue) symbolizing purity and readiness for death, sedge hat (sugegasa), walking staff (kongōzue) representing Kōbō Daishi himself, stole (wagesa), prayer beads (juzu), and name slips (osamefuda) deposited at each temple.

At each temple: bow at the gate, purify at the water basin, ring the bell, offer incense and candle at the Main Hall, chant the Heart Sutra (Hannya Shingyō), repeat at the Daishi Hall, receive the stamp. The full sequence takes 20-30 minutes per temple.

Charitable gifts from locals to pilgrims — food, drinks, money, or lodging. A deeply rooted tradition in Shikoku. It is customary to accept osettai graciously and give a name slip (osamefuda) in return.

The walking staff is considered an embodiment of Kōbō Daishi. It must be washed and placed in the room before the pilgrim washes. It must never be placed on the ground where it could be stepped over. In historical times, if a pilgrim died, the staff was used as their grave marker.

etiquette.en: Remove shoes before entering temple buildings.
Do not ring the bell when leaving a temple — only upon arrival.
The walking staff must never be used to point or gesture.
Accept osettai graciously, even if you do not need it. Refusing is considered disrespectful to Kōbō Daishi.

relevance.en: The definitive English-language book on the Shikoku pilgrimage. Statler walked the route multiple times and documented its history, culture, and personal meaning.
Translations of Kūkai's major philosophical and religious writings. Essential for understanding the spiritual foundation of the pilgrimage.

description.en: Celebrated at Temple 75 (Zentsū-ji), Kūkai's birthplace. Special ceremonies and increased pilgrim activity.

historicalSignificance.en: The Shikoku pilgrimage is one of the few circular pilgrimages in the world and the most important Buddhist pilgrimage in Japan. It embodies the Shingon Buddhist concept of spiritual progression through four stages: awakening, discipline, enlightenment, and nirvana — mapped onto the four provinces of Shikoku.

notes.en: Open year-round but rainy season (June-July) and typhoon season (August-September) are challenging. Winter walking is possible but some mountain temples may be difficult to access. Peak seasons are spring (cherry blossoms) and autumn (fall foliage).

accommodationNotes.en: Shukubo (temple lodging): 6,000-8,500 JPY with meals. Minshuku: 5,500-7,500 JPY with meals. Business hotels: 3,000-8,000 JPY without meals. Cash is essential — credit cards rarely accepted at traditional lodging. Advance reservations recommended, especially in Kochi (long gaps between temples).

cellularNotes.en: Good coverage in towns and along main roads. Weak or no signal on mountain trails, particularly in remote Kochi sections.

waterNotes.en: Vending machines are ubiquitous in Japan, even in rural areas. Carry extra water for the long mountain sections in Kochi and southern Ehime.

notes.en: Nearest airport to Temple 1. Bus to Naruto/Bando area.
Trains circle most of Shikoku. Useful for reaching temples or skipping difficult sections. JR passes available.
